note: Weak masculine (n-declension): takes -en in akk/dat/gen sg (den/dem/des Studenten). This is the 'den Kunde -> den Kunden' bug we flagged.
Mixed declension: oblique sg is Namen (weak-like), but genitive is Namens (-ns). The genitive cannot be derived from the weak rule, so it is an explicit override.
Fully irregular neuter. nom/akk sg = Herz, but dat sg Herzen and gen sg Herzens break every rule -> explicit per-form overrides. Tests the escape hatch.
Regular masculine. Tests genitive -es (Tages) and dative-plural -n (Tage -> Tagen).
Regular neuter with -er plural. Tests dative-plural -n on an -er plural (Kinder -> Kindern).
Regular neuter with -s plural. Tests the dative-plural exception: plurals ending in -s do NOT take -n (Autos -> Autos, not Autosn).
Feminine. Tests that feminine nouns take NO genitive -s (der Frau, not der Fraus).
Umlaut plural (Zug -> Züge). IMPORTANT: the plural is a stem change, not a suffix, so an 'ending-only' blank cannot represent it. Surfaces the limit of ending-fill mode.
Feminine umlaut plural (Stadt -> Städte). No genitive -s; plural is a stem change.
